description: Apollo 13 Flight Journal - Day 6, part 4: The Last Course Correction

tokens: Only 10 hours remain to the splashdown of Apollo 13.

The crew has received all the instructions needed to reawaken their Command Module Odyssey, plus instructions on Lunar Module separation, required to be ejected before the crew's return to Earth in the heatshield-protected Command Module.
Although the LM still serves fine as their shelter, the crew is starting to show signs of the extreme fatigue caused by their situation.
Everyone is trying to catch some rest now, before the stress of the reentry.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 131 hours, 47 minutes.
Apollo 13 now 66,596 nautical miles from the Earth travelling at a speed of almost 7,500 feet per second at the present time, and we're some 10 hours, 53 minutes from reentry.
5 hours, 53 minutes from the scheduled mid-course correction maneuver, mid-course correction 7, which is targeted to put Apollo 13 in the center of the reentry corridor.

Our last communications with the spacecraft was at 129 hours, 39 minutes when we completed passing up the Lunar Module preentry checklist to Fred Haise, and we presume that all three of the crewmen have been resting or at least relaxing since that time.
At 128 hours, 17 minutes or about 3½ hours ago Fred Haise and - rather Jack Swigert and Jim Lovell were scheduled to begin a rest period.
Although, we did hear from Jim Lovell once or twice, after that time, we have not heard from Jack Swigert since the rest period began.
At 131 hours, 49 minutes; this is Mission Control, standing by.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Distance to go, 123,336 km, velocity, 2,286 m/s.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 132 hours, 29 minutes...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Undiscussed on the air to ground radio loop, Fred is feeling worse for wear.
He has developed a urinary tract infection as a result of insufficient water intake.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lovell, from1970 Technical debrief:"Fred woke up with chills before we did midcourse correction 7."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I wasn't sure what gave me the chills.
I was back in the CM at about that time, and I had to go to the bathroom.
I stripped naked in the 42 degree temperature ricocheted around touching bare metal, and it just chilled me to the bone every time I'd touch anything.
You can't help but bounce all around in there.
I was really cold for the next 4 hours.
From that time on, it sort of began to catch up with me.
Before that, I really didn't feel much effect at all."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Fred, in a couple of minutes we're going to hand over the Honeysuckle, so there'll be - Our uplink will be terminated.
We'll contact you when we get it back.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: They are passing the MSFN duty from the station in Goldstone, California to Honeysuckle in Australia, leading to a momentary break in communications.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: After that, I've got a couple minutes of work for you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Let's wait until after the handover.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes, I'm hearing you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We want to get some ranging data on you for about 15 minutes.
So close the Power Amplifier circuit breaker and go to Normal Voice, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Once again, the radio system is reconfigured to support the ranging function.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You ought to have it now, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: You're coming through good now, Fred.
I've got three Deltas to the checklist.
I'd like to pass them along now.
One of them is to the CSM checklist.
So if you'll pick that up, I'll give it to you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Is it the one Jack wrote them all in?
He also wrote something in the G&N book.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: It's in the big, long one he wrote out at about EI minus 02:30.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: He's coming back down with it.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Meanwhile, Fred, I've got two for you on the LM Prep checklist.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I've got a couple of changes for you on your CSM checklist.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The time line between EI minus 02:30 and 1 hour is real crowded, so you're really going to have to hustle, and we've decided to delete the EMS entry check at minus 02:30.
That'll give you a little more time in there.
What we've decided to do with it is wait until after EI minus 1, just before you initialize the EMS.
If you have time, and only if you have time, do the EMS entry check.
Otherwise, forget it and go right on into EMS initialization.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You're going to delete between 2 hours and 30 minutes and 02:15 there, and you're going to add it, if there is time, only if there is time, at EI minus 1 hour where the EMS initialization occurs.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Just before EI minus 2 hours and 15 minutes, we need an E-memory dump Verb 74 Enter.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Right before 2 hours and 15 minutes, I assume that's before MSFN gives their P27 update, I do a Verb 74.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Mission Control wants a look at the erasable memory before the computer update, and will require a Verb 74 to downlink its contents.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: How much sleep did you get, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: It was awful cold, and it wasn't very good sleep.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: You plan to try to get any more?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Well, if I - If I get everything done, I'll try, but I tell you, it's almost impossible to sleep.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: All of us have that same problem.
It's just too cold to sleep.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The way we're looking at it, looks like you ought to have a couple free hours here before you have to really get with it.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We'll - we'll take it easy, but I - and we'll try to sleep, but it's just awful cold.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Present data indicates that your entry angle is minus 6.03 degrees.
Your Delta-V at the midcourse is going to 2.8 feet a second.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I understand this is going to be an RCS burn.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: It's going to be an RCS burn.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The final midcourse correction will be so small that using the RCS jets will be the best way to achieve it, rather than the DPS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we need the Suit Circuit Relief valve back to Auto.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And one thing, comparing Jim's checklist with my own, I find one difference there at EI minus 02:30.
His checklist has me changing a - opening a EPS Sensor Signal circuit breaker right prior to turning off LM power.
Is - I'd like to find out which checklist is correct.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The LM checklist is just for information to Jim.
It's just to tell him that - that you're changing back to Command Module power.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: And when do you figure that you'll be sending up the PADs that you have?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Aquarius, all of your PADs will be coming up in about 3½ hours, at EI minus 06:30.

And one thing we want to remind you of, when you remove power from the Command Module LM umbilical, is to be sure that you open the LM Power Main B circuit breakers, both of them, before you start throwing switches and circuit breakers in the LM.
Do it as we've outlined it in the procedure, in that order.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: That's the way we planned on it.
We'll let Jack do his three and then he'll tell us when we're Go from there.
What pages are those changes on?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: The one I've got for you is at the end of power removal from the Command Module LM umbilical.

And just for your information, after you go through that entire procedure and about the time you're ready to transfer to the Command Module, at that time, power will be removed from the umbilical, and it's okay to disconnect it.
That is, after you've thrown the switches in the LM.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: That's where I've got a remark here to check with you to see if it's all right to proceed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And that would be just after, on panel 16, you opening Ascent ECA Control breakers.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The confusing thing is, Jack, where they told me to wait for MSFN Go before proceeding was that Bat 2 Low Volts Off/Reset, then On.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We'll give you a go after waiting 5 seconds, and then down from there just a few steps, after you have opened the Ascent ECA Control breakers on panel 16, at that point, the umbilical is not powered, and it's okay to disconnect them should you care to do so.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: After the last step then, I'm free to disconnect.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And one other change we have is, shortly after that, where we're configuring for jettison, we have closed both the Forward Dump valve and the Overhead Dump valve.
And we don't want to get in a locked up position like that, so one way to get around it is to - After we close the Forward Dump valve, turn the Descent Oxygen valve off.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: After the Forward Dump valve, add a step in that says the Descent Ox valve, off.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: What Lousma is probably saying here is that without the dump valves active, the cabin pressure might rise unexpectedly due to no longer having the capacity to automatically vent excess pressure.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And perhaps you heard that our Gammas are still minus 6.03 and our Delta-V will be a 2.8 foot per second RCS burn.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Freddo, we've got one more change we'd like to give you to clarify a question that Jack asked earlier.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I meant to call Jack back down.
I'm not familiar with the previous discussion.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: At E mi - EI minus 02:30, during power removal from the umbilical, first thing we do is two steps with the CSM.
We open the Main B, the LM Power to Main B circuit breakers, and then there's a third step which says "Circuit breaker EPS Sensor Signal, Main B, open." Just delete that step.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The confusing order to disable the EPS telemetry monitoring is removed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Aquarius, for your information, as far as our water supply is concerned, including our plans for powerup, we have an additional 18 hours of water remaining from this point.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Eighteen hours of water remaining from this point, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I just want to make sure that you're filtering the changes to the checklist that come up to make sure that they're absolutely essential.
When we learn our procedures, we can only do it one time, and we can't make changes at the last minute.
We'd like to do the best, the safest way possible, but unless the changes are really essential, don't bother sending them up.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim has returned to the airwaves with a further demand that their workload has to be managed, and not cluttered with last minute changes.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The chairman of the CPCB is still active, and one thing we're trying to do is to save you all the time we can between EI minus 02:30 and 1 hour.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: For your information, Jim, I don't know whether you heard that originally, but that time from 2½ to 1 in there has been run about three times, and it's pretty tight, so we've tried to weed out what we could, which isn't much, but I think the other message you might impress on Jack when you get around to aligning that platform, don't try to get it down too neat.
It doesn't have to be all that good.
Just do a nice quick and dirty one, and that's going to be good enough anyway.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: I think for reentry we don't have to have a real accurate platform, but I haven't told Jack that.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Deke's suggestion of not working too hard on the platform alignment might be an offer to reduce their workload, but might not be what the perfectionist crewmembers want to hear, even under the current circumstances.
On the other hand, the re-entry attitude isn't critical as it is only setting up the CM in approximately the correct attitude which the aerodynamic forces will take it to anyway.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Jim, while you're up and things are nice and quiet, let me give you a couple of other things to think about.

I know none of you are sleeping worth a damn because it's so cold, and you might want to dig out the medical kit there around 01:35 or in that ballpark, and pull out a couple of Dexedrines apiece and try one about then, and another around 01:39 to 01:40.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: It is well known to Mission Control that the crew has not been sleeping properly ever since the accident.
It is a great concern to them that they might be getting too exhausted to properly work through the improvised reentry procedures.
Hence comes the suggestion via Deke that they should take some of the dextroamphetamine stimulant tablets stored in the medical kit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, I hadn't brought that up.
We might - We might consider it.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Slayton, Director of Flight Crew Operations interjecting some comments to the crew from a CapCom console.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Wish we could figure a way to get a hot cup of coffee up to you.
It'd probably taste pretty good about now, wouldn't it?
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: You don't realize how cold this thing becomes when it's in a PTC mode that's slowing down, and I just clocked the cycles on my [garble] And it's about 11 to 12 minutes now, and the Sun is directly overhead, so it's shining on the engine bell of the Service Module and not getting down to the spacecraft at all.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Dehydrated coffee would have been carried, but when made with the cold water they have available, it surely would not have the morale-raising effect of the hot beverage suggested by Deke Slayton.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As a matter of fact, doing this alignment on the Earth this time will be like making a landing with a fogged-up windshield.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 133 hours, 11 minutes.
At 132 hours, 28 minutes; CapCom Jack Lousma put in a call to the crew.

Fred Haise responded and we had him turn on the power amplifier which addition to greatly improving the quality of voice communications we get, also gives us ranging data which the Flight Dynamics Officer's using for making final computations of the midcourse correction to be performed in about 4½ hours.
Jack Swigert reported that he received - gotten about 2 to 3 hours sleep which he said was not very good sleep due to the cold in the Command Module.
Swigert said it is almost impossible to sleep because of the cold and Donald K.

Slayton, Director of Flight Crew Operations, and who has been at the CapCom console a good part of the evening and into this morning, came on the circuit and advised Jim Lovell to consider taking Dexedrine tablets for each of the crewmen.
This is a stimulant contained in the medical kit.
Lovell said that they had considered that and would think about it.
At the present time, Apollo 13 is 60,249 nautical miles from the Earth.
The spacecraft velocity is up to 7,900 feet per second and we're some 9 hours, 27 minutes until reentry.
We'll continue to stand by for any further communications with the crew.
We don't have any plan to pass up to them in the next few minutes or so.

One of the things under discussion, however, at the present time in Mission Control are procedures which might possibly be used to increase the temperature in the Lunar Module and the LM environmental and electrical systems engineer's looking into the power status right now.
As far as consumables are concerned, with the possibility in mind that perhaps bringing some additional equipment on line in the LM to bring the temperature up there.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Distance to go is 111,581 km, their velocity is up to 2,408 m/s.
In the hour and 7 minutes since the last PAO update, they have travelled 12,000 km and accelerated a further 122 m/s.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're presently showing the cabin temperature in the Lunar Module at about 54 degrees, and we although do not have a measurement on the Command Module we presume that it is lower than that, and we'll continue to keep you advised on the status of the discussion going on at present in Mission Control on the advisability of powering up the LM more fully to bring up the temperature.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: LM temperature is around 12 degrees Celsius.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We figured out a way for you to keep warm.

We decided to start powering you up now, and what we want you to do - what we want you to do is take your entry LM Prep checklist and start at the top where it says "Bat 5 Normal FEED on," and then jump over as it says to your 30-minute activation, and do all of the 30-minute activation up to, but not including, the burn.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: If I understand you correctly, then that gives me leeway to maneuver when we get up to activation complete, and we can be in position for the burn, but we will not burn.
We don't have a PAD anyway.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: You could maneuver to burn attitude, or you could maneuver to an attitude which should put the Sun in the windows to warm the place up.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Sounds good, and you're sure we have plenty of electrical power to do this?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We've got plenty of power to do it.
I can get you a number, though.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jim, you've got about 100-per cent margins on everything from here on in.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: We're not talking about the CSM right now.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: To relieve the crew's miserable conditions, the Go has been given to start up the LM early.
Having active equipment will warm up the cabin and give the crew a couple more comfortable hours before reentry.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This short turn on step 3 on page 24 has us only turning on the RCS System A/B, two quads, and the breakers are still out on panel 11.
Did you want those in, too, or are we just going to use one set of heaters?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Go as the checklist recommends for now.
When you get into the circuit breaker panel configuration, you're going to get the number 1 set of heaters on anyway.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 133 hours, 34 minutes.
We're following the progress of the crew as they power up the Lunar Module, according to the checklist, and at the present time we show the total average current...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We'd like to get a hack to set our mission timer here, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: You've got about 30 seconds to set it.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'll give you the 2-second delay in there.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're showing about 34-35 amps total current now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The crew has proceeded with their power-up and tripled their power usage.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: One other way to warm things up in a hurry in there is when you get your AC on to turn on the window heaters.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I guess the only question I have is what it's liable to do to us with the - looks like almost a frost on it now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'd like to let it maybe warm up just a little bit more before hitting it with the heat load.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And, as you can see, Houston, at turn-on we got our old friend downlink 2 back.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: 'Downlink 2' refers to their present communications configuration, which includes the ranging on as present.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It looks like you're proceeding toward the gimbal lock there.
We'd like you to check that, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Not much we can do about it.
We can't use the thrusters, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Forget it now; we'll get it later.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Without the RCS thrusters powered, they won't be able to rectify their drift towards a possible gimbal lock situation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I guess the next thing for the PGNS will be a coarse align 000, but I guess we ought to hold up now until we get enough time on the RCS thrusters.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We'll give you the Go on them.
Your quads are 120 to 133 now, so you're cleared for - Cleared for thrusters.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The RCS thruster quads measure from 48 to 56 degrees Celsius.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Did you get my Go for RCS?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: What we're doing now, Jack, is letting the spacecraft drift in this mode to pick up the Earth again.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: I don't want to just go blasting around the sky and get high rates, because I don't have anything to null the rates on until the Earth comes back up again.
And - Once I get the Earth in sight, we have no strain on nulling rates.
That part of the Earth, that is.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim is probably referring to them having no inertial reference going.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It's going to be interesting today, Jack.
The Earth's a lot bigger; the crescent is a lot more pronounced than it was yesterday.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Well, you're going in the right direction.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 133 hour, 55 minutes.
Jim Lovell has stabilized the spacecraft at this point using the LM Reaction Control System thrusters.
We're showing a power level on the Lunar Module right now ranging between 45 and 50 amps.

The power-up procedure followed by the crew will have another effect in addition to warming up the LM, and that is they're beginning a portion of the preentry checklist that had been scheduled for 6 hours prior to Entry Interface, and that was begun at about 9 hours.
So, they'll be getting a bit of a leg up on that portion of the time line.
At the present time, Apollo 13 is...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And, Jack, I guess we haven't changed our angle much with respect to the Sun 93 million miles away, so it ought to be in about the same place in the AOT, isn't that Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'll get an answer for you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: 'Charlie' is not a person but military lingo for 'clear' or 'confirmed'.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Aquarius, your ASA is warmed up now.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The Abort Guidance System (AGS) gyroscopes and accelerometers in the ASA (Abort Sensor Assembly) have reached operational temperature.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When you look out a detent 2 in the proper burn attitude, what you ought to see is the Sun at 12 o'clock, about halfway between the top of the AOT and the center of the pipper.
And you ought to see the Earth...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And, it's getting a little warmer in here now.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Duck blinds are always warmer, Jim, when the birds are flying.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Jack, I've gotten so used to flying attitude with the TTCA, I won't be able to do it normally.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: I said I gotten so used to flying attitude with the translational control, I won't be able to do with the ACA.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Their unusual configuration resulted in Jim and Fred having to learn a new way to fly the spacecraft stack, and soon he will have to try to revert to the normal way of going at it once the Service Module is gone.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Aquarius, we see your glycol temperature getting up there.
If you want to make it a little warmer, or you can try putting your Suit Temp valve to High, if you haven't already got it there.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Their Suit Temp has been sitting on Cold for quite some time, to try and make the cabin warmer by having the cooling system remove less heat from the spacecraft.
Now that they are powering up the systems, the system is working in a more normal manner and they ought to adjust the temperature control to its usual position to raise the temperature.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Aquarius, something we're thinking about right now is, if we can do it without using a lot of RCS, it would be to our advantage timewise to try to get an alignment.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'll see what we can do, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And we'd planned to use the Moon and the Sun for that.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Although Mission Control had not planned to bring up the LM's computer up again, the opportunity has presented itself.
By taking sightings of the Moon and the Sun, they can coarse align the IMU to a known inertial orientation (in a Program 51 alignment) and then proceed to a 52 for the fine alignment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On panel 16, we'd like for you to close the Cross Tie Bal Loads breaker, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And it looks like we could support a - an alignment in a few minutes, if you'd be willing to go ahead with that.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I think from our position here, we know where the Sun and Moon are and it's strictly going to be a pitch maneuver.
But I think we can save some gas.
I'll see what we can do.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Are you going to ship us up a REFSMMAT?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: It's shining in the rendezvous window.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And, Aquarius, before we can ship you a load, we'll have to have, on panel 11, under Comm, the Updata Link circuit breaker closed.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The familiar updata power supply has to be turned on before they can receive the computer update.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And it'll be a few minutes yet, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Stay with it, Jack, and I'll go back to data.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I got Bat in about 33⅓ per cent on that uplink.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I just got another uplink too fast when I took the Data switch back off - It's happening, I guess, about a third of the time.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We're ready with your load if you'll give us Data please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: They are hitting the switch to allow Mission Control to upload data into their newly restarted LGC.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: After the uplink, you'll have to set the drift and the REFSMMAT flag as on page 8 of the contingency book, steps 5 and 6.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Are you done with the computer now, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Aquarius, we're through with it, it's your computer.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: And, Aquarius, Houston; take option 1 on a P52 when you get to it.
And I've got some ball angles for a Sun and Moon.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We want an option 1 and you've got some ball angles for Sun and Moon.
And right now, Jim has the Sun pretty well squared away right in the middle of AOT.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Option 1 is the 'Preferred' alignment option and will take their then-present attitude and use it as an attitude reference.
Taking sightings of the Sun and the Moon will help them to refine the alignment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 134 hours, 42 minutes.
The crew began powering up the Lunar Module at about 133 hours, 29 minutes.
A little over an hour ago, we've since seen the currents in the Lunar Module come up from about 10 to 12 amps to their present level of about 40 amps.

These currents actually went to a high of about 70 amps before stabilizing out at the lower level as the heaters came on line and brought the equipment up to a proper temperature and then dropped off-line.
The crew reported it is getting somewhat warmer within the Lunar Module at this time.

The cabin temperature reading that we have on the ground is based on the temperature of the glycol and water boiler, which is related to the amount of heat being transferred into the water boiler, and is about the best indication we have of cabin temperature.
And that shows that the cabin temperature now has come up from about 54 degrees to about 56 degrees.
This temperature, however, does show some lag and we would expect the cabin temperature has come up probably a bit more than is indicated by the temperature we're showing on the ground.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The cabin temperature is from 12.2 to 13.3 degrees Celsius, and just a little more comfortable than earlier.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And have you got some plan in vectors for us, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: For the Sun, I've got 246; for the Moon I've got 250.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Are you talking about ball theta angles?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I was - I was reporting the code for Noun 70.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We have the Sun marks and I'll start up at pitch now to go over and pick up the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Just as a note of interest, in this dock configurations for P52s, the Command Module docking probe is right down the middle of - the docking light, rather, is right down the middle of the detent.
And when the Sun flashes on, it really makes it difficult.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 134 hours, 56 minutes.
In Mission Control at this time, we're in the process of handing over shifts.
Flight Director Gene Kranz is taking over from Flight Director Milton Windler.
We will not have a change of shift briefing.
The Flight Director plans to remain in the Control Center through splashdown, and we therefore, as I said, will not have a change of shift briefing - news briefing for this shift.
In summarizing briefly the events during the past 8 hours, from about 127 hours until about 130 hours, we passed up the Command Module and Lunar Module preentry checklists to the crew.
Following this, the crew was advised to attempt to get some rest.

Fred Haise remained on watch, but we limited the number of calls to the spacecraft to allow Fred also to get some rest, and about 128 hour, 17 minutes, Jim Lovell and Jack Swigert indicated that they were going to begin a rest period.
Fred Haise began a rest period at about 129 hours, 39 minutes.
At 132 hours, 28 minutes, we put in the first call to the crew after Haise began his rest period.
All three crewmen responded shortly thereafter, and Jack Swigert reported that they had gotten about 2 or 3 hours of sleep.
They said that it was not very good sleep, that it had been quite cold, and almost impossible to sleep.
Deke Slayton, Director of Flight Crew Operations who has been in the Control Center during the night and early morning advised Lovell at that point to consider taking dexedrine tablet.
This is a stimulant carried in the medical kit.
Lovell said he would consider it.
We've heard no report from the crew at this time as to whether they have or not taken any medication.
In response to the crew comments on the cold, we began looking at some methods of bringing the LM temperatures up.

It was decided that we had adequate power margins in the LM batteries and also adequate water margins to power up the LM early, and this had been planned to occur at 6 hours prior to reentry, and we began the procedure about 3 hours earlier than that.
Beginning to power up at about 9 hours prior to Entry Interface.

At 133 hours, 29 minutes; the crew began the checklist procedures to power up the LM and the current levels in the LM came up from about 10 to 12 amps, which is the normal power down, current level to about 70 amps, and then as heaters brought equipment up to temperature, and the heaters began to drop off-line, the temperature stabilized out at about 40 amps, and the crew reported the temperature is coming up within the LM and we've seen a corresponding rise in temperature on our displays here.
The temperature we were reading in the cabin prior to the power up was about 54 degrees since the equipment has been turned on, we've seen the temperature come up about 2 to 3 degrees.

At the present time, we're 7 hours, 40 minutes, 54 seconds from Entry Interface, and 2 hours, 40 minutes, 51 seconds from the mid-course correction, Correction number 7, which is planned to occur 5 hours prior to reentry.
The Flight Dynamics Officer is computing final maneuver PAD for this mid-course correction.

The preliminary information on it was that it would be about 2.8 feet per second using the LM ascent stage - actually the LM Reaction Control System thrusters, and burning them for about 21 seconds duration.
We expect that we will get an update to this when the Flight Dynamics Officer completes the final computations for the maneuver.
We will also have some preliminary numbers for the entry.
We suspect that these will change once the mid-course correction is completed.
These are the same numbers which were passed out at the previous change of shift briefing.
They are as follows: We are predicting Entry Interface at a point in which the spacecraft reaches the 400,000 foot level to occur at 142 hours, 40 minutes, 40 seconds.

The drogue chutes would deploy at 142 hours, 48 minutes, 53 seconds, and the main chutes, the main parachutes would come out at 142 hours, 49 minutes, 43 seconds with splashdown at 142 hours, 54 minutes, 40 seconds.
As I said, we expect these times will shift somewhat when the mid-course correction has been completed.
We also have the times for the beginning of blackout and the end of blackout.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The time we show for the beginning of blackout is 142 hours, 40 minutes, 58 seconds; and time for ending of blackout is 142 hours, 44 minutes, 3 seconds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Well, Jack, that's what it says the torqueing angles are.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Aquarius, hold on to torqueing angles, Please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jim, the reason for the delay is that we're not seeing the data yet.
We're having to check a point here; and as soon as they come up, we'll let you know what to do with them.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We had a large Noun 05 of - what - 112, and our torqueing angles, Jack, are minus 01713, minus 03278, minus 01395.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Minus 01713, and we see them now.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Noun 05 displays the angular differences, or the degree that the gimbals have to be torqued to get the IMU to the best possible alignment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Aquarius, do you have a star close by there you could check?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I was just trying to get a check on the Moon, again, to see if those angles were indeed true, and we got the Moon back again and centered.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: What you're reading now the angles?
We had the Moon centered, and it's pretty close to what we have on the 8 ball, I guess.
Close enough for any entry that we'd like to do.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And I'm told that Denubla - Denebola and Regulus are nearby if you wanted to make a star check.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm going to start pitching around again, and I'll see if I can pick them up.
I have Orion out here to my left a little bit, but it's pretty close to the - oh, here.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim proceeds to use star sightings to check the accuracy of their alignment.
By having the computer try to point them at the stars in question, they will find out if the platform is good.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 135 hours, 11 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 13.
That was Apollo 13 Commander Jim Lovell talking with capsule communicator Jack Lousma.
While 13 - Apollo 13 is aligning the platform in the Lunar Module, our digital displays now show Apollo 13 at 50,905 nautical miles away from Earth.
The spacecraft velocity reads 8,670 feet per second.
We're at 135 hours, 12 minutes continuing to monitor; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Aquarius, is Jack sitting on the rumble seat there?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: He just headed upstairs to take another look around.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I got a minor addition to the entry checklist for him.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: He has that in his pocket.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: What I'm doing, Jack, is just - I'm pitching over now.
I'm going to pick up another star.
Sirius was just too far off.
I thought I was going to use too much gas getting there.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: By the time I get aligned in the - in the AOT - be nice if we didn't, have Odyssey attached, we could just Auto - Auto maneuver over to these things.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Looks to us like you've got her aligned, Jim, so I wouldn't worry about it too much.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm pretty confident that the platform's fairly decent.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We need an E Mod Verb 74, when you've got a chance, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Verb 74 performs the erasable memory dump, to allow Mission Control to read the contents of the LGC's memory.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On your entry checklist, on page 2-5.
Down there on step 9, where it says 152 degrees pitch at .05g, adjacent to that, so that Recovery can see you better on the way down, we want you to turn your S-Band, Power Amplifier to High.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Turn S-Band, Power AMP to High at .05g time.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Switching the power amplifier to High will attempt to maintain radio signal with recovery forces for as long as possible.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; 135 hours, 27 minutes now into the mission.
We're 2 minutes - 2 hours, 12 minutes away from scheduled time of ignition for MCC-7.
And our space digital design show 13 - Apollo 13 - at a distance of 49,577 nautical miles out from Earth, travelling at a speed of 8,790 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're considering doing the midcourse with PGNS, unless you'd rather do it in AGS.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I just align myself up with the old ball again.
So I've got you foresighted again, but any way you want to do it.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Like you say, you might as well go first class.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I just lost a lot of friends there.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The crew has been joking that using the primary guidance system means 'going first class'.
Since they unexpectedly have PGNS running, they can use primary guidance for attitude control.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Jack, you can tell Owen Morris that the RCS System A/B 2 Quad 1 breaker is still nicely in.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: One of RCS breakers was broken before launch.
The permanently closed circuit breaker is still, indeed, closed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That was Fred Haise talking to Jack Lousma.
Owen Morris referred to in that conservation, is the deputy manager for the Lunar Module in the Apollo Spacecraft Program Office, here at the Manned Spacecraft Center.
We now show Apollo 13 at 48,822 nautical miles away from Earth, and at a velocity of 8,861 feet per second.
At 135 hours, 37 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We think we've figured out a way to save you some time at a very critical - very full schedule.
And that's by doing a docked coarse align, since we got the LM up now.
That would save you a maneuver or two.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: It seemed to me a docked coarse align might be quicker for - for Jack.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We think it would be, and it'd save quite a bit of time at a place where you're going to be pretty busy.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're looking at doing this in the Service Module Sep attitude, and the optics will be pointed away from the Sun.
So it should be a good attitude for a P52.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: So, we'll be going to the Service Module Sep attitude, at which time we'll do a docked coarse align, and...
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Then you want - You want Jack then to do a P52?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The way we'll do that in our time line Jim, is to go ahead and do the Service Module Jett and then we'll just stay in that attitude and when it comes time in our time line as we've outlined, to bring the platform up, we'll proceed with the P52 - coarse align, and then the P52.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Are we going to use the same techniques that we normally do for LM activation?
In other words, I try to maintain an attitude, and give him some angles and then - Are you going to give him the angles?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Basically, it's the same procedure just reversed, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Another nice thing about this, is it's one we've done before.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The sudden availability of the LM's guidance computer and the IMU has given them the opportunity to reverse the standard procedure of docked IMU alignment, where the Command Module supplies the LM's initial IMU alignment.
Nothing prevents them from doing the same procedure the other way around, although the calculations will be somewhat different.
As they say, this would be faster than doing the earlier alignment idea.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: One thing, however, that we do not plan to do is to proceed with the Command Module power-up prematurely.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I just wanted to talk over with you, it looks like we've had some changes in the Flight Plan here, due to Jim's P52.
Do you have - Can you talk over with me what your plans are?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Since we've got the PGNS up, we plan to use that information to give the CMC a dock coarse align, and then we're in the Service Module jettison attitude, we'll wait until it comes time to power up the CMC, and we'll get the CMC a dock coarse align, and we'll pick some good stars to give you a fine align with, and it looks like we can pick some stars that are looking away from the Sun in which you can find in that Service Module jettison attitude; so, we'll save you quite a bit of gas, and save you some time in a very busy time.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Both the crew and Mission Control are enthusiastic about their new faster way of aligning the Command Module's platform for reentry.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, it's warmed up here now.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I'm looking out the window now, Jack, and that Earth is whistling in like a high-speed freight train.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: That's Command Module Pilot Jack Swigert describing the temperature and Apollo 13, as you copied.
The comfort level is going up.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're clocking you at 48,000 miles and coming in at about 9,000.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: They're 88,900 km away and their velocity has climbed up to 2,743 m/s.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I don't think there's many LMs that have seen it like this.
I'm still looking for Fra Mauro and Cone Crater.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: You're going the wrong way, son.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Slayton who came on the CapCom line to point out to Jim Lovell that they're headed the wrong way for Fra Mauro and Cone Crater.
Our displays now show Apollo 13 at 47,858 nautical miles out from Earth.
We're at 135 hours, 48 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 13.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The crew's improved mood can even be heard in the little jokes.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It looks - Just looking over what I may expect here, it looks like I'm just going to get three angles to do a Verb 41 Noun 20, right?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's what it looks like from here, Jack.
It's pretty much the opposite of the LM activation procedure where we do the dock coarse align.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes, except in a way we did a lot of Verb 06 Noun 20, Enters, simultaneously, and then you all shipped him up post-torque values.
You're not going to do anything like that are you?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: During the activation part, we do a lot of Verb 06 Noun 20, Enters simultaneously, reading you out the difference in the angles, and then MSFN furnishes the post-torqueing angles, in order to get the platform fine aligned.
Do you plan something like that or just three coarse align angles.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jack, we're going to give you three coarse align angles, and then you can go right to your checklist as we're giving it, and start in with the Verb 40 Noun 20.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The procedure on page 7-1 would normally be used to realign the IMU using attitude information derived via the Gyro Display Coupler (or GDC) from the two BMAGs in the Command Module.

The way they are planning to use it here, however, is to input values based on the alignment of the LM's IMU, which are then converted by Mission Control to be useful in the Command Module's different orientation.
As noted by the crew, it is the reverse of the procedure to transfer guidance information from the CM to the LM, which was done during the entry into the lifeboat Aquarius.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; now 135 hours, 52 minutes now into the mission.
Our displays in Mission Control indicate the Lunar Module cabin temperature now getting up to around 60 degrees.
The spacecraft currently running around 40 amps.
This is as compared to 10 to 12 amps when 13 was in a powered down state.
As you recall, we brought the Lunar Module...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And, Jack, how do you read?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: As you recall, we brought the lunar...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, I was around shooting pictures of all the debris inside here, before we left, and I inadvertently changed the settings on the DC Command Module Reseau camera that we need for the Service Module pictures.
I wonder if FAO can dig them out again - what we need, f-stop and speed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We brought the Lunar Module power up about 3 hours early this morning, because Apollo 13 now has the luxury of margins in both power and water.
This does give an added bonus.
It puts the 13 crew a step ahead in what could be considered a very busy time line.
We're now at 135 hours, 54 minutes into the flight.
We show Apollo 13 at 47,312 nautical miles away from Earth, velocity now reading 9,010 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Fred, in regards to the camera settings, for black-and-white 3400 film, the settings were f:5.6 at 1/250th.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'd guessed right after all, but thank you.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I got MCC-7 PAD when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We're in luck, I got one PAD left.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We'll take care not to change this one.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: MCC-7: 137:39:48.39, minus 0003.1, plus all zeros, plus 00001, N/A, plus 0020.5, 0003.1, 0:23, 008, 359; the rest is N/A.
Remarks: plus-X, four jets, RCS; and your weights for the DAP load: LM weight, 25181; CSM weight, 62468.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: MCC-7: 137:39:48.39, minus 0003.1, plus all balls, plus 0000.1, N/A, plus 0020.5, plus 0003.1, 0:23, 008, 359; the rest of pad N/A.
Remarks: plus-X, four jets, RCS, ullage; the LM weight, 25181; CSM weight, 62468.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: With the PGNS back in action for this burn, the PAD information returns to the original format used for LM crew maneuvers.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:A midcourse correction to improve their entry corridor angle.Time of ignition (Noun 33):137 hours, 39 minutes, 48.39 seconds.Change in velocity, fps (m/s) (Noun 81):x, -3.1 (-0.95 m/s); y, 0.0 (0 m/s); z, +0.1 (0.03 m/s).
The change in velocity is resolved into three components which are quoted relative to the LVLH (Local Vertical/Local Horizontal).HAandHPApogee is not applicable, since this is a reentry trajectory.
Perigee is 20.5 nautical miles or 38.0 km.
This brings the closest point of approach to the surface of the Earth within the atmosphere, which means that the spacecraft will reenter.Delta-VR:3.1 fps (-0.94 m/s).

This is the total resultant change in velocity the spacecraft would experience and is a vector sum of the three components given above.Burn duration or burn time:23 seconds.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 0008°; Pitch, 359°.
The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform and is therefore set using the FDAI display in its inertial mode.
Unlike a CSM PAD, yaw is not stated for the LM.
Procedures for a lunar landing allowed the spacecraft to yaw around the thrust axis as required for operational reasons.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Other notes include that this burn will be performed using the LM RCS plus-X thrusters, a four-jet ullage, and DAP with the weights of the docked spacecraft specified.
Guidance will be on PGNS and the IMU with AGS backup.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I have a Service Module Sep PAD if you want to copy that, now.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I have a Service Module Sep PAD with the attitudes.
You don't need a PAD sheet for it; just any old blank sheet will do.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I was going to say I don't - We don't hardly carry a Service Module Sep PAD.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes, we'll have to change that.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The PAD reads as follows and then I'll repeat the angles for you so you can copy them.
The following MCC-7, maneuver the LM to the following FDAI attitudes: roll, 000; pitch, 91.3; yaw, 000.
Now do you want those attitudes repeated, Fred?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Following MCC-7 we're to maneuver to the following attitudes: roll, 000; pitch, 091.3; yaw, 000.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And the last part of the PAD is at GET 138:10:00, which is EI minus 4.5 hours, execute a push of 0.5 feet per second, four jet, plus-X; perform SM Sep; then execute pull, 0.5 feet per second, four jet, minus-X.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: This is the push-pull maneuver, which will change their velocity briefly and put a little bit of distance between the two components.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jack's entering the Command Module, now.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: This understated exchange signifies an important mark - Swigert has moved into the Odyssey to start the power up according to the reentry checklist.
His first duty will be to configure part of the power system and start the RCS thruster heating.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That last Joe was execute at the GET at 138:10:00 which is EI minus 4½ hours; execute a push of 0.5 feet per second, four-jet ullage; then execute SM Sep followed by a pull of 0.5 feet per second, with respect to a nomenclature on the TTCA; I think we really need an up of 0.5 and then a down of 0.5.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: The last PAD I had for you right now is the LM jettison PAD.
Similar to the - Stand by 1, Aquarius.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Request P00 and Data for a data load.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And I was about to say the LM jettison PAD is similar to the SM Sep PAD, Fred, when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Just about the same number of lines?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Prior to 141:40:00, which is EI minus 1 hour, maneuver the LM to the following FDAI angles: roll, 130; pitch, 125; yaw, 012.4.
The corresponding CSM gimbal angles will be roll, 291; pitch, 196; yaw, 045, and that's the PAD.
And the computer is yours, Aquarius.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: A LM Sep PAD prior to 141:40:00, EI minus 1 hour, maneuver to following attitudes: roll, 130; pitch, 125; yaw, 012.4.
The corresponding CSM gimbal angles are roll, 291; pitch, 196; yaw, 045.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: It is important to know the Command Module's gimbal angles, since their platform will be functional at the time of the separation, and risks gimbal lock if the middle gimbal angle goes past 90 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston; now 136 hours, 15 minutes now into the flight.
That maneuver PAD for midcourse 7 it was passed up.
Shows that the time of ignition of 137 hours, 39 minutes, 48.39 seconds; with a Delta-V of 3.1 feet per second and a burn duration of 23 seconds.
Jim Lovell reported Jack Swigert entering the Command Module, and we copied that time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're so efficient down here that we got an entry PAD ready, Fred.
Do you want to copy that for Jack?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I'll have to try to borrow his book from him.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Can we hold off on that a little bit, Joe?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: I just wanted to let you know that we had it.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I hope that when you send up all those uplinks to Jack that you could get them up to him quickly.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We're shooting for less than 5 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And are you still using the computer?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We copied Swigert entering the Command Module at 136 hours, 10 minutes, 49 seconds...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And, Fred, the computer has your target load in.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Joe Kerwin, by the way, is taking over the position as capsule communicator in the Mission Control Center.
Well, in the Command Module Swigert will be warming up some of the systems.
Throwing the circuit breakers and main bus B and some of the heaters for equipment onboard the Command Module.
Almost at the same time as Swigert entered the Command Module, Ken Mattingly came in the control center still in an apparently spotless condition.
We're at 136 hours, 18 minutes into the flight.
We show Apollo 13 at an altitude of 45,255 nautical miles, traveling at a speed of ninety-two hundred, two hundred and twenty two [likely means 9,222] feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I just want to clarify one thing on the LM Sep PAD.
It appears to me that in my configuration, I could probably use a Verb 49 loading in 622, yaw, pitch, and roll, in that order; and then being able to fly out at 5018 in roll, pitch, and yaw.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim refers to using the automatic maneuver to get to the separation attitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stand by; I'll verify it, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And while you're doing that I've got a question about the Command Module checklist
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Either I copied the circuit breaker wrong, or - I can't read it.
Comes down just about the - oh, about the 20th one down, after panel 276, where it says CB Instrumentation Power Control 3 and 4, open.
The next circuit breaker on panel 5 - I - Would you give that to me again?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's CB Essential Instrumentation Power, Main B.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I just can't, right - I just can't read my writing, Essential Instrumentation Power Main B, closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jack is powering up the onboard sensor equipment, but is having trouble with his own penmanship.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The word we have is that you can't make a Verb 49 maneuver to the LM jettison attitude because those are FDAI angles we gave you, they don't correspond to the gimbal angles for the load; it'll have to be a manual maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And mind out for gimbal lock.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston at 136 hours, 26 minutes now into the flight.
Our clock in Mission Control shows the time of ignition for the midcourse burn at 1 hour, 14 minutes from this time.
30 minutes after the midcourse, or 4½ hours prior to time Entry Interface, the Service Module is scheduled to be jettisoned.
At that time Jack Swigert will be in the Command Module.
Jim Lovell and Fred Haise in the Lunar Module.
Lovell will fire the Lunar Module thrust to push the Service Module about ½ foot per second.
Swigert, then, activates the pyros with a switch in the Command Module.
After separation, Apollo 13 will back off with the Lunar Module Reaction Control System at ½ foot per second providing a separation Delta velocity of 1 foot per second.
All three crewmen will be trying to take pictures of the Service Module at that time.
Swigert out of window number 5 in the Command Module.
Lovell and Haise out of the Lunar Module.
For separation, Apollo 13 will pitch about 90 degrees along the radial axis.
That would be 90 degrees off the flight path angle.
And at time of Entry Interface, or entry into the Earth's atmosphere, the Command Module and Service Module should be more than 16,000 feet apart.
We're at 136 hour, 28 minutes into the flight.
We show Apollo 13 at a distance of 44,395 nautical miles, and a velocity of 9,312 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're looking at LM current, to see if Jack has started his preheat, and we haven't seen it yet.
Is he doing okay down there?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Houston, Jack said he's already started it, and he said that in 1 more minute, he'll be up to 20 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Have him let us know what his test meter reads when he's done.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: He says that he had a Battery A voltage drop of 2 volts, and he'll try to look at the test meter for you right now.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Because only one half of the thrusters can be heated using power from the LM umbilical, the rest are heated using power from Battery A.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: He's been looking at, them, but they haven't been coming up, so far.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Do you see a current now, Houston?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: That's affirmative, Jim; it looks like we are seeing one now.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And thanks for keeping us on it.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Reminder P41 for the RCS burn.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We got to protect our jobs, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We've been DPS-ing so long here.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Joe is reminding the crew to use Program 41 in the Lunar Module computer, which is the RCS Burn program.
Their previous PGNS-controlled DPS burns were performed using Program 40.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The flight control team here in Mission Control monitoring the display noted that Jim Lovell had punched his onboard computer into program 40 which is the thrusting program for the Descent Propulsion System.
Then came the reminder to go to program 41 for a Reaction Control System of mid-course burn.
We show that we're 51 minutes, 50 seconds from time of ignition with Apollo 13 at a distance of 42,599 nautical miles away from Earth with a velocity of 9,515 feet per second.
That's 51 minutes, 30 seconds now from time of ignition for mid-course - for the mid-course burn.
This is Apollo Control, Houston at 136 hours, 49 minutes now into the flight.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I finished up the maneuvers, the Auto maneuver in 41; but my roll and yaw needles seem to be offset.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We recommend PGNS Mode Control to Att Hold.
Save a little gas and stand by on the error needles.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Figured, if we're going to do this burn in PGNS now, I ought to give you an update on the contingency book pages 32, 33, and 34, because the last time we went through this portion, we were burning it in AGS.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We've been talking about possibly having you do it in AGS.
We recommend at this time that you do an AGS to PGNS align, the 400 plus 3 procedure only.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Inputting400 + 3000into the DEDA will have the AGS align its own inertial reference to the IMU.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We recommend that you perform this burn in AGS, as you did the last midcourse maneuver; we think it will save gas.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Joe, do you want me to line up the same way we did the last one, too?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: That was capsule communicator Joe Kerwin, passing along to Jim Lovell the recommendation to perform the midcourse burn with the Abort Guidance System.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And, Joe, Jack just handed me some injector temperatures, if you want to read these on [garble] so I can plug them in.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: 5 Charlie, 4.0; 5 Dogs, 3.7; 6 Able, 3.5; 6 Bravo, 4.1; 6 Charlie, 4.2; 6 Delta, 3.8.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: The temperatures are read from the Systems Test Meter, and they range from + 22°F to +34°F, or -5.5 °C to 1.1 °C.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Our recommendation on this burn is that you maneuver to the burn attitude, in PGNS Min Impulse, then do a body-axis align, 400 plus 5, followed by 400 plus 0, and then do the burn in AGS.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Inputting400 +50000into the DEDA aligns the AGS' inertial reference to the body axes of the LM.
Inputting400 +00000sets up the AGS attitude hold.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now we're spinning it with the PGNS, what you gave us for a PGNS attitude.
Do you want me to just to align up the Earth as I did before in the last midcourse?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We don't want you to do that.
Read me your FDAI angles, and let's compare them with what we have down here.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: You're looking at them in the DSKY.
I've got roll of 8.47; pitch of about 0.51; and yaw looking at about 3.750.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Those are very close - I guess all you need to do is trim them up a bit.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I plan to do a final trim - Auto trim, and then a four-jet translation.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: For fuel conservation, we'd prefer you to trim it up Min Impulse, and there's really very little trim required; and then go ahead and do it AGS.
We're on the expected fuel usage, but we're just being old ladies about it.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: My only question, Joe, is the fact that both the roll and the yaw needles did not go to null when I did an Auto maneuver.
I'd tried to go manually to the attitude; and then went to Auto, but the roll and the yaw do not come in at all.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: I haven't got an answer on that yet, but your attitude looks very close.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I'm not sure that if I follow and null the needles, that will be the proper attitude.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: It's going to be very close; in any event, close enough.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And, Jim, just for your information, I have the entry PAD.
I have the landing area weather summary, which you probably don't even have to copy, but which I'll read up to you when you're ready; and some stars for Jack that I'm holding for him for later on.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And, Joe, Jack says all the injector temperatures, he just checked them again, and they're all over 3.9...
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: 137:13:49 Unknown MCC Speaker:...If he knows the needles...
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Understand, that they're all over 3.9 now.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: You can tell him that it's looking good to us.
They were even happy with the previous ones.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 137 hours, 15 minutes now into the flight.
We show a time of ignition of - for the midcourse burn a little over 25 minutes away now.
This will be a burn with a Delta V of 3.1 feet per second.
It's a relatively long duration on the burn...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And, my only question, Houston, is do you want me to null the PGNS needle manually?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: ...relatively long duration on the burn because of the large spacecraft weight at this time.
We show the spacecraft weight at 87,649 pounds at the present.
Again the midcourse burn will be done retrograde.
We're at 137 hours, 16 minutes into the flight.
We show Apollo 13 at 40,081 nautical miles away from the Earth and travelling at a speed of 9,814 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I guess the basic question is comparing the balls here and out the window it doesn't look too unreasonable, and I guess Jim says the COAS is yawed and roll slightly off from what he might eyeball; but yet, the FDAI air needles for PGNS are showing a full scale left in roll and full scale left in yaw.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: It appears if we track those we, obviously, aren't going to be on the attitude that we burned the last midcourse.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Well, this attitude we passed you is not quite aligned to the terminator, and stand by on this.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: It should be off about 8 degrees, Flight.
transcript.speaker: Flight

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 137 hours, 20 minutes into the flight.
A goodly gathering of the astronaut corp in the Mission Control Center now, Donald K.
Slayton is here, as is Tom Stafford, Chief of the Astronaut Office.
Charlie Duke is here, Ken Mattingly, Gene Cernan in addition to Joe Kerwin, immediately around the CapCom console.
On the first row is Ron Evans and Tony England.
Meanwhile, in the back is Jim McDivitt, manning one of the positions at the management console.
Jim McDivitt, as you recall, is the manager of the Apollo Spacecraft Program Office at present.
We're at a distance now of 39,635 nautical miles away from Earth.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Ronald Evans joined NASA in Astronaut Group 5 in 1966.
He had so far worked in the support crew for Apollo 7 and Apollo 11 and as a CapCom for Apollos 7, 9 and 11.
He was the backup CMP for Apollo 14.
He would be the CMP for Apollo 17 in 1972.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're - we're all agreed that you're almost, but not quite, in the proper attitude, and we're just trying to get you one firm recommendation on how to proceed from here.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I have nulled the null - roll needle and the pitch needle now and I'm yawed - rolled left now.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The yaw isn't, but the pitch and roll are.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Your attitude really looks quite good except in roll, and we'd like you to do the following.

In Min Impulse PGNS, we'd like you to trim to zero pitch, which is about where you are now; to 008 degrees in roll, which is about 16 degrees from your present roll attitude; and to zero degrees in yaw, which is about where you are now.
Then we'd like you to do the body-axis align 400 plus 5, 400 plus 0.
Pitch and Roll to Pulse, select AGS, do the burn in AGS.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I'm rolled the wrong way; that is what you're saying, right?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And you know I can't roll in minimum impulse.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Less than 15 minutes away now from the scheduled time of the midcourse burn.
137 hours, 25 minutes into the flight and Apollo 13 now 39,206 nautical miles away from Earth with a speed of 9,927 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And you don't want me to automatically trim 5018, I take it.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We just want you to cycle on through P41 to get the Average G and burn it out.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Average G is a routine in the computer which calculates the state vector during powered flight, updating it every two seconds based on the acceleration being measured.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Just for your information, we see 3.0 in register 1.
We read you up 3.1 on the PAD.
The actual Delta-V was 3.05, and they warned me that it might come out 3.0.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We will give you a mark at 10 minutes to the burn, which is in 28 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We'll burn it in AGS and use the AGS ball.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Despite planning the maneuver using PGNS computer guidance, the crew has elected to use the AGS instead.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lovell, from1970 Technical debrief:"We finally got our rough alignment for midcourse correction 7.
Houston called up and asked if we would like to do a PGNS burn, and we said 'Fine.' That's where I really got confused.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lovell, from1970 Technical debrief:"I really preferred to do the old AGS burn again, because we had done it one time and I knew that it worked.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Haise, from1970 Technical debrief:"That ended up being an AGS burn, Jim."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lovell, from1970 Technical debrief:"Yes, Houston finally told us to go to the AGS."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I was looking at the wrong clock, and we're a little under 10 minutes now.
I'll give you a mark at 9 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: That was Joe Kerwin confirming to Jim Lovell, spacecraft commander, that spacecraft attitude for the burn does look good.
We're at 137 hours, 30 minutes into the flight.
Now showing Apollo 13 at 38,700 nautical miles away from Earth, current speed 9,990 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Their distance is 71,672 km; velocity, 3,045 m/s.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We have 10 seconds until 9 minutes to the burn.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: That agrees with our event timer.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Apollo Control coming up on 6 minutes to time of the midcourse burn.
This burn performed will be a backup guiding system aboard Aquarius.
The burn retrograde will provide a delta velocity of 3 feet per second and with a predicted burn duration of 21 seconds.
We're at 137 hours, 34 minutes into the flight, and this is Apollo Control, Houston.
That burn duration is 23 seconds, not 21 seconds.
We're at 137 hours, 35 minutes into the flight.
We show Apollo 13 at 38,303 nautical miles away from Earth, and with a velocity of 10,045 feet per second.
Less than 5 minutes now away from scheduled time of ignition.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: About 3 minutes to go, and we're all squared away.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And we're standing by for your body-axis align and your zeroing 404, 405, 406, going to 470.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: These set up the AGS to monitor their acceleration for the burn, and then display it on the DEDA.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You'd asked - you'd asked me before, Joe, to go 400 plus 3, which I did.
I assume you've changed the script again.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Less than 2 minutes away now from scheduled time of ignition.
We're at 137 hours, 38 minutes in the flight.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We did tell you to do that awhile ago, and it doesn't matter; you're looking good.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: It looks like we had a minus 0.2 bias at 470.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Fred Haise reporting that the burn is underway.
We're at 137 hours, 40 minutes into the flight.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You're good right where you are.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin
